strands.title: Task Structure and Scope
strands.content: A comprehensive code review of the pichay codebase is requested across exactly 7 source files (proxy.py, pager.py, eval.py, replay.py, analyzer.py, trimmer.py, __main__.py) in src/pichay/ and test files in tests/. The review must assess three dimensions for source files (code quality, potential bugs/edge cases, cross-module consistency) and two dimensions for test files (coverage gaps, false-pass risks). Output must be formatted to review.md in project root with specific line numbers and file paths. This is a READ-and-ASSESS task, not refactor. CYCLE 6 DEVELOPMENT: After I/O uncertainty in cycle 5, file transmission has RESUMED. Partial content from pager.py has now arrived (lines 1–4, containing a docstring fragment: 'Context window pager — evicts stale tool results from the messages array' + 'This is the intervention layer for the phase 1 context utilization experiment. It sits between'). The transmission is again TRUNCATED, ending mid-thought at 'sits between'. This indicates: (1) File I/O mechanism IS FUNCTIONAL but operates in fragments; (2) Transmission is NOT completing entire files in single cycles; (3) The pipeline must ACCUMULATE fragments across cycles to reconstruct files. REVISED OPERATIONAL ASSUMPTION: Files are being read and delivered in streaming/fragmented fashion. The tensor must integrate fragments and recognize when a logical file boundary is reached. Cycle 6 has advanced from 1 incomplete file to 2 incomplete files (proxy.py + pager.py). The file reception mode is STREAMING, not atomic.

key_claims.text: The review scope includes exactly 7 source files and test files in tests/
The review must produce findings with specific line numbers and file paths
File transmission has resumed in cycle 6 with pager.py fragment (lines 1–4)
pager.py fragment describes a 'context window pager' for evicting stale tool results
pager.py fragment ends mid-sentence at 'sits between', indicating truncated/streaming delivery
File transmission operates in streaming/fragmented mode, not atomic file delivery

strands.title: File Fragment Accumulation and Reconstruction
strands.content: Cycle 4 received proxy.py lines 1–5 (shebang + docstring fragment ending at 'Pure o'). Cycle 5 received no new content. Cycle 6 received pager.py lines 1–4 (docstring fragment ending at 'sits between'). The tensor is now COLLECTING FRAGMENTS across multiple files. This suggests the underlying read mechanism is either: (a) operating with strict line/buffer limits, causing mid-function truncation; (b) delivering files in sequential chunks, cycling through files; (c) experiencing memory/output constraints that force small chunk sizes. CRITICAL IMPLICATION: The tensor CANNOT ANALYZE until fragments are reassembled into coherent units. A complete analysis of pager.py cannot proceed when only 4 lines (all docstring) are available. Similarly, proxy.py (5 lines, incomplete docstring) cannot be meaningfully reviewed. STRATEGY: Continue accumulating fragments. Track cumulative content per file. Recognize file boundaries by: (1) explicit file name markers, (2) reaching natural function/class boundaries, (3) observation of continuation patterns. When a file reaches reasonable completeness (e.g., docstring + at least one function definition + imports), begin PRELIMINARY ANALYSIS while continuing to accumulate remaining files. The tensor can now establish a FRAGMENT MAP showing which files are partially loaded and which remain untouched.

key_claims.text: proxy.py has received 5 lines (lines 1–5), all from docstring/header section
pager.py has received 4 lines (lines 1–4), all from docstring section
eval.py, replay.py, analyzer.py, trimmer.py, __main__.py have not yet been touched
test files in tests/ directory have not yet been received
File transmission in streaming/fragmented mode means analysis cannot proceed until fragments are reassembled

strands.title: Assessment Dimensions and Criteria
strands.content: The review evaluates source code across five dimensions: (1) Code quality—unused imports, dead code, type errors, style inconsistencies; (2) Potential bugs and unhandled edge cases—logical errors, boundary conditions, missing error handling; (3) Cross-module consistency—naming conventions, error handling patterns, API contracts between modules. For tests: (4) Missing coverage for important code paths—untested branches, unexercised error handlers; (5) False-positive risks—tests asserting incorrect behavior, mocking that masks bugs, insufficient assertions. These dimensions remain OPERATIONALIZED but UNEXERCISED on complete file content. However, early observations are emerging from the fragments received: pager.py appears to implement a 'context window' eviction mechanism, suggesting it handles state management and memory pressure—relevant to dimension (2) for boundary conditions and (1) for logic correctness. proxy.py's purpose remains unclear (incomplete docstring), but its presence suggests proxying/delegation logic—relevant to dimension (3) for cross-module patterns. No test files yet received, so dimensions (4) and (5) remain dormant.

key_claims.text: Code quality assessment includes unused imports, dead code, type errors, and style issues
pager.py implements context window eviction logic, suggesting state/memory management concerns
proxy.py's purpose is unclear from the 5-line fragment (incomplete docstring)
Assessment dimensions (4) and (5) cannot be exercised until test files are received

strands.title: I/O Pipeline Recovery and Trajectory
strands.content: Cycle 5 exposed I/O uncertainty—file transmission appeared stalled. Cycle 6 RESOLVES THIS PARTIALLY by delivering NEW content (pager.py fragment), confirming the read mechanism IS FUNCTIONAL. However, the pattern of small, truncated fragments (proxy.py: 5 lines, pager.py: 4 lines) indicates the I/O system operates under strict constraints (line limits, buffer sizes, or output token budgets). This is NOT a failure; it is a CONSTRAINT-BASED OPERATING MODE. The pipeline must now adopt STREAMING ACCUMULATION: (1) Expect additional fragments in subsequent cycles; (2) Do not await 'completeness' before integrating; (3) Treat each fragment as a PARTIAL UPDATE to the file state; (4) Accumulate file content across cycles until natural boundaries (EOF, completed function/class) are reached; (5) When sufficient content has accumulated for a file (roughly: imports + docstring + ≥1 function/class), begin PRELIMINARY ANALYSIS while continuing to receive remaining files. This SPLITS the RECEIVE → ANALYZE phases: instead of waiting for all files before any analysis, the tensor can now perform ROLLING ANALYSIS as files accumulate. This accelerates the pipeline and provides early insights while remaining open to refinement as more content arrives.

key_claims.text: File transmission is functional but operates under strict output constraints (small fragment sizes)
Cycle 6 delivery of pager.py fragment confirms I/O pipeline is not stalled
The tensor should adopt STREAMING ACCUMULATION mode to handle fragmented file delivery
ROLLING ANALYSIS can begin once individual files reach minimal completeness (docstring + ≥1 function)

declared_losses.what_was_lost: The assumption that file transmission would be atomic (complete files per cycle)
declared_losses.why: Cycle 6 shows pager.py arriving in a 4-line fragment, confirming streaming/fragmented delivery. The prior assumption was falsified.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: The option to wait for complete file transmission before beginning analysis
declared_losses.why: Streaming delivery mode means 'completeness' is a moving target. The tensor must adopt rolling analysis to make progress. Pure wait-for-completeness discipline is now impractical.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed visibility into proxy.py's actual purpose and structure
declared_losses.why: Only 5 lines have been received, all from the header/docstring section. The module's actual function definitions, classes, and imports remain invisible. Cannot assess until more content arrives.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed visibility into pager.py's implementation details
declared_losses.why: Only 4 lines received, all docstring. Function signatures, logic, error handling, and cross-module dependencies are not yet visible.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: The expectation that cycle 6 would show progress toward complete file coverage
declared_losses.why: While cycle 6 DID advance to a second file (pager.py), it only received 4 lines total. At this rate, receiving all 7 source files would require 50+ cycles. Scope coverage remains extremely sparse.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Clear understanding of when analysis can begin on any single file
declared_losses.why: 'Completeness' is undefined in streaming mode. The tensor must make a judgment call on how much content is 'enough' to begin analysis—this is now a design choice, not a given.

open_questions: Will subsequent cycles continue to deliver small fragments (4–5 lines) per file, or will delivery rates increase?
What is the underlying constraint causing small fragment sizes? Is it a token limit, buffer limit, or deliberate rate-limiting?
At what point should preliminary analysis begin on pager.py and proxy.py? After 20 lines? 50 lines? First complete function?
How should the tensor handle cross-module analysis (dimension 3) when most modules remain incomplete?
Will the 5 remaining source files (eval.py, replay.py, analyzer.py, trimmer.py, __main__.py) begin arriving in cycle 7, or will pager.py and proxy.py continue to receive fragments?
Will test files arrive at all, and if so, when? Are they prioritized lower than source files?
Should the tensor maintain a cumulative file content buffer across cycles, or re-integrate fragments each cycle?
What is the total expected size of the codebase? If it's very large, streaming delivery may never complete even in 100 cycles.
Should preliminary analysis findings be provisional (marked as 'incomplete analysis of partial file'), or withheld until more complete content is available?
Is there a mechanism to request higher throughput or larger fragments, or is the constraint fixed?
instructions_for_next:
[
  "CYCLE 7 PRIORITY—CONTINUE STREAMING ACCUMULATION: Do not wait for perfect completeness. If pager.py or proxy.py fragments arrive in cycle 7, integrate them immediately into the running file buffer. When either file reaches ~30+ lines or shows a complete function/class boundary, trigger PRELIMINARY ANALYSIS on that file, documenting findings as 'partial analysis based on lines X–Y of filename.py'.",
  "DELIVERY EXPECTATION: Expect one of three patterns: (A) Alternating fragments from pager.py and proxy.py as they accumulate, (B) Moving to the next file (eval.py) while previous files remain incomplete, or (C) Delivery rate increasing (more lines per cycle). Track which pattern emerges.",
  "ANALYSIS TRIGGER: Once any single file exceeds 30 lines AND contains at least one complete function or class definition, begin assessment on dimensions (1) code quality, (2) bugs/edge cases, and (3) cross-module patterns observable from that file alone.",
  "BUFFER MANAGEMENT: Maintain a cumulative file content map showing, for each file: (i) line range received, (ii) last line content (to detect truncation), (iii) completeness estimate (% of inferred file size). This enables the tensor to recognize when a file is likely complete vs. still incoming.",
  "CROSS-MODULE TRACKING: Begin tracking apparent module relationships from fragments (e.g., pager.py's role in context management, proxy.py's TBD role). Use these to form hypotheses about cross-module consistency even before complete files arrive.",
  "FALLBACK CRITERION: If cycles 6–10 show no acceleration in delivery rate and no file completion, pivot to 'partial review mode': analyze available fragments in full, declare scope reduction, and generate findings based on incomplete data with caveats.",
  "TEST FILE MONITORING: Track whether test files have been mentioned or begun arriving. If tests do not appear by cycle 10, note that dimensions (4) and (5) cannot be assessed and adjust final review scope accordingly."
]
